description: CNRY Diagnostic Intelligence Platform — synthetic knowledge base for proof-of-concept testing. 50 symptom clusters covering common recreational marine inboard diesel/petrol engine problems.

entries.symptom: Low rumbling noise from gearbox area that worsens when shifting into reverse
entries.frequency_signature: low frequency, 80-200 Hz

remedies.action: Check and replace gearbox oil — often milky or very dark if contaminated
remedies.notes: Fixes this in over half of cases. Ten-minute job at a haul-out.

remedies.action: Inspect and replace gearbox coupling / flexible drive plate
remedies.notes: Worn coupling causes direction-specific rumble. Common on Volvo Penta and Yanmar drives.

remedies.action: Check input shaft bearing for wear
remedies.notes: If oil change doesn't resolve, bearing wear is next suspect.

entries.symptom: Steady vibration at idle that disappears above 1500 RPM
entries.frequency_signature: resonance at idle RPM band

remedies.action: Inspect and replace engine mounts — look for cracked or collapsed rubber
remedies.notes: Most common cause of idle-specific vibration. Mounts deteriorate with age and oil contamination.

remedies.action: Check for one misfiring cylinder at idle — compression test all cylinders
remedies.notes: A dead cylinder at low load causes rough idle vibration that smooths out under load.

remedies.action: Check propeller for bent blade or barnacle fouling
remedies.notes: Prop imbalance vibrates worse at specific RPMs.

entries.symptom: High-pitched whine from engine bay that increases with RPM
entries.frequency_signature: high frequency, pitch scales with RPM

remedies.action: Inspect drive belts for wear, glazing, or misalignment — replace if cracked
remedies.notes: Glazed or slipping belt is the most common cause of RPM-proportional whine.

remedies.action: Check alternator bearings — spin by hand with engine off, listen for roughness
remedies.notes: Failing alternator bearing produces a whine that tracks engine speed.

remedies.action: Inspect raw water pump impeller and housing for wear
remedies.notes: Worn or air-starved raw water pump can whine under load.

entries.symptom: Engine overheating — temperature gauge rising above normal, alarm triggered

remedies.action: Replace raw water pump impeller — check for missing vanes in housing
remedies.notes: Impeller failure is the most common cause of marine engine overheating. Annual replacement recommended.

remedies.action: Flush and descale heat exchanger — check for blockage with flow test
remedies.notes: Scale and salt deposit build-up restricts coolant flow, especially in hard water areas.

remedies.action: Check thermostat — remove and test in hot water, replace if stuck closed
remedies.notes: Stuck thermostat prevents coolant circulation. Easy cheap fix to rule out early.

remedies.action: Inspect raw water strainer for blockage — weed, plastic, debris
remedies.notes: Blocked strainer starves the pump. Check first before anything else.

entries.symptom: Knocking or rattling sound from engine — metallic, rhythmic, linked to RPM
entries.frequency_signature: rhythmic knock, frequency proportional to RPM

remedies.action: Check engine oil level and pressure immediately — do not run engine
remedies.notes: Big-end or main bearing knock sounds similar to this. Running without oil causes catastrophic damage.

remedies.action: Check for loose heat shield or exhaust manifold bolts
remedies.notes: Loose metal against engine casing rattles rhythmically with RPM and is often mistaken for internal knock.

remedies.action: Inspect injectors — have them tested for spray pattern and opening pressure
remedies.notes: Diesel knock from a faulty injector is a sharp tap rather than a deep rumble.

entries.symptom: Black or excessive smoke from exhaust under load

remedies.action: Check and clean or replace air filter — restricted airflow causes rich running
remedies.notes: Most common cause on older engines. Easy first check.

remedies.action: Have injectors tested and cleaned — worn injectors drip and cause incomplete combustion
remedies.notes: Dripping injectors show as black smoke under load with power loss.

remedies.action: Check turbocharger seals if turbocharged — oil in intake causes heavy black smoke
remedies.notes: Turbo seal failure lets oil into air charge, producing thick black smoke.

entries.symptom: White or blue smoke from exhaust, worse on start-up, clears when warm

remedies.action: Check coolant level and inspect for head gasket failure — look for mayo in oil cap
remedies.notes: White smoke that persists when warm often means coolant entering combustion. Check before running further.

remedies.action: Check valve stem seals — blue smoke on start-up that clears is classic worn stem seals
remedies.notes: Oil sits on valve stems overnight, burns off on first start — producing blue smoke that then clears.

remedies.action: Inspect wet exhaust mixing elbow for cracks or blockage
remedies.notes: Cracked mixing elbow can let water into exhaust and appear as white smoke.

entries.symptom: Loss of power — engine feels sluggish, won't reach normal WOT RPM

remedies.action: Check propeller for fouling, bent blade, or incorrect pitch
remedies.notes: A fouled or damaged prop is the most common cause of power loss that doesn't show any other symptoms.

remedies.action: Replace fuel filters — primary and secondary
remedies.notes: Partially blocked fuel filter starves engine under load. Easy cheap fix.

remedies.action: Check for air leak in fuel system — look for bubbles in clear fuel lines
remedies.notes: Air ingress causes power loss, especially under load. Lift pump diaphragm failure is common.

remedies.action: Inspect exhaust system for partial blockage
remedies.notes: Collapsed mixing elbow or blocked silencer causes severe back-pressure and power loss.

entries.symptom: Vibration felt through helm and throttle lever, not present at neutral
entries.frequency_signature: vibration increases with throttle, felt through controls

remedies.action: Inspect propeller for bent blade or strike damage — have it balanced
remedies.notes: Most likely cause, especially if onset was sudden. Any prop strike can cause imbalance.

remedies.action: Check cutlass bearing for wear — prop shaft should have no lateral play
remedies.notes: Worn cutlass bearing allows shaft to wobble, causing vibration through hull and controls.

remedies.action: Check prop shaft alignment with engine — measure coupling faces for gap
remedies.notes: Misalignment puts cyclic load on shaft and causes vibration that gets worse under load.

remedies.action: Inspect stern tube and stern gland for wear
remedies.notes: Worn stern tube allows shaft movement, especially felt as vibration under power.

entries.symptom: Clicking or tapping from engine top end — faster when RPM increases
entries.frequency_signature: tapping, frequency doubles with RPM on 4-stroke

remedies.action: Check and adjust valve clearances to manufacturer specification
remedies.notes: Excessive valve clearance is the classic cause of tappety top-end noise. Clearances open up as engines age.

remedies.action: Check oil pressure and oil level — low oil pressure starves rocker gear
remedies.notes: Insufficient lubrication to top end causes tapping. Check before assuming valve adjustment needed.

remedies.action: Inspect rocker arms and pushrods for wear or a collapsed follower
remedies.notes: A collapsed hydraulic follower or worn rocker arm tip causes a persistent tap on one cylinder.

entries.symptom: Engine starts fine but stalls under load or when throttle is advanced quickly

remedies.action: Replace primary fuel filter and bleed air from system
remedies.notes: Partial blockage allows idle running but engine starves at full demand.

remedies.action: Check for water in fuel — drain fuel separator bowl
remedies.notes: Water contamination causes stalling under load as it reaches injectors.

remedies.action: Inspect lift pump — diaphragm failure causes intermittent fuel delivery
remedies.notes: Worn lift pump delivers enough fuel at idle but can't keep up at higher demand.

entries.symptom: Clunk or thud when engaging gear — worse when gearbox is cold
entries.frequency_signature: single clunk on engagement

remedies.action: Adjust throttle-to-gear engagement sequence — engage at lower RPM
remedies.notes: Engaging at high idle causes clunk in any gearbox. Check operator habit first.

remedies.action: Change gearbox oil and check gearbox pressure — many boxes have a clutch adjustment
remedies.notes: Fresh oil and correct clutch pressure reduces engagement shock significantly.

remedies.action: Inspect flexible coupling between engine and gearbox
remedies.notes: A worn coupling allows rotational shock to transmit directly to gearbox on engagement.

entries.symptom: Steady 220-300 Hz vibration signature at idle that increases slightly with RPM
entries.frequency_signature: 220-300 Hz, present at idle, slight increase with RPM

remedies.action: Inspect gearbox input shaft bearing — remove gearbox and check for roughness or pitting
remedies.notes: A 220-300 Hz signature at idle on a diesel is characteristic of early gearbox bearing wear.

remedies.action: Check all engine accessories for bearing wear — alternator, raw water pump, idler
remedies.notes: Accessory bearings produce similar frequency range. Isolate by temporarily removing belts one at a time.

remedies.action: Inspect intermediate shaft bearing if fitted
remedies.notes: P-bracket or strut bearing wear produces a resonant vibration in this frequency band.

entries.symptom: Diesel engine hard to start when cold, takes many cranks, blows white smoke on first start

remedies.action: Test and replace faulty glow plugs — use a glow plug tester, replace all if one fails
remedies.notes: Failed glow plugs are by far the most common cause of cold-start difficulty on diesel.

remedies.action: Check compression on all cylinders — low compression causes hard cold starts
remedies.notes: Worn rings or valves reduce compression, making cold starts difficult. Engine often runs fine once warm.

remedies.action: Check battery condition and starter motor — slow cranking worsens cold-start on diesel
remedies.notes: Diesel needs fast cranking to build compression heat. Marginal battery causes hard cold starts.

entries.symptom: Oil pressure warning light flickering at idle — goes out above 1000 RPM

remedies.action: Check oil level immediately — top up if low and retest
remedies.notes: Low oil is the first check. Never run engine with oil pressure warning showing.

remedies.action: Replace oil pressure sender/switch — they fail and give false readings
remedies.notes: Faulty sender is very common and often mimics real pressure loss. Cheap and easy to replace.

remedies.action: Change engine oil and filter — check if oil is very old or heavily diluted with fuel
remedies.notes: Degraded oil loses viscosity, causing low idle pressure. Diesel dilution from excessive idling worsens this.

remedies.action: If above don't resolve, check oil pump wear and main bearing clearances
remedies.notes: Genuine low pressure at idle with good oil level suggests worn pump or excessive bearing clearance — specialist work.

entries.symptom: Propeller cavitation — boat loses grip, stern lifts, foam trail visible
entries.frequency_signature: high frequency cavitation noise, irregular

remedies.action: Inspect propeller — look for damage to leading edges and erosion pitting
remedies.notes: Damaged leading edges cause cavitation at speed. Have prop refurbished or replaced.

remedies.action: Check propeller depth and check if hull appendages are directing aerated water to prop
remedies.notes: Hull gear (transducers, anodes, planing strakes) ahead of prop can feed it aerated water at speed.

remedies.action: Review propeller pitch selection — too much pitch causes cavitation at high speed
remedies.notes: Overpitched prop is over-loaded and cavitates at WOT. Check against engine manufacturer's WOT RPM range.

entries.symptom: Water in bilge coming from stern gland area — drips when engine running

remedies.action: Adjust traditional stuffing box gland nut — a small drip when running is normal, tighten if excessive
remedies.notes: Traditional packed glands should drip 1 drop per minute when underway. Tightening a quarter turn usually resolves excess dripping.

remedies.action: Repack stern gland with fresh packing cord — flax or PTFE
remedies.notes: Old packing hardens and either drips or seizes. Repacking every 2-3 years is normal maintenance.

remedies.action: Replace with dripless shaft seal if traditional gland is worn beyond adjustment
remedies.notes: PSS or similar dripless seals eliminate dripping entirely and are retrofit to most boats.

entries.symptom: Alternator not charging — battery voltage dropping, charge light on

remedies.action: Check drive belt — broken or slipping belt means no charge output
remedies.notes: First check. A broken belt is immediately visible. Slipping belt smells burnt.

remedies.action: Check alternator connections and earth — corroded terminals cause charging failure
remedies.notes: Marine environment corrodes connections quickly. Clean and coat terminals with protective spray.

remedies.action: Test alternator output with multimeter — should read 13.8-14.4V at idle
remedies.notes: If voltage is low at battery with good belt and connections, alternator regulator or diodes are suspect.

remedies.action: Replace alternator — rebuilt units are often cheaper than new
remedies.notes: Failed diodes or regulator inside alternator — usually not worth repairing. Replace unit.

entries.symptom: Grinding noise when propeller shaft rotates — worst when underway, less at idle
entries.frequency_signature: grinding, continuous when underway

remedies.action: Replace cutlass bearing — check for lateral play in shaft at P-bracket or stern tube
remedies.notes: Worn cutlass bearing allows shaft to grind against bearing housing. Should have no lateral play.

remedies.action: Inspect shaft for scoring or pitting at bearing contact points
remedies.notes: A scored shaft will destroy replacement bearings quickly. May need polishing or replacement.

remedies.action: Check for rope, line, or fishing gear wrapped around shaft
remedies.notes: Fouled shaft is very common and causes grinding when rotating. Always check before assuming mechanical failure.

entries.symptom: Engine revs surge and hunt at idle — RPM rises and falls rhythmically without throttle input

remedies.action: Check for air leak in fuel system — particularly lift pump and filter connections
remedies.notes: Air ingress causes hunting as fuel supply varies. Look for bubbles in clear fuel lines.

remedies.action: Clean or replace idle speed control / throttle body if petrol engine
remedies.notes: Dirty IAC valve or throttle body causes hunting on petrol engines.

remedies.action: Check governor linkage and springs on diesel engine — inspect for wear or sticking
remedies.notes: Sticky or worn governor causes RPM hunting on mechanical injection diesels.

entries.symptom: Excessive exhaust water — water pouring from exhaust even at rest

remedies.action: Check mixing elbow for failure — cracked or collapsed elbow floods the exhaust with water
remedies.notes: If engine is not running and water is entering through exhaust, siphoning via mixing elbow is suspect.

remedies.action: Check anti-siphon valve on raw water circuit — should be fitted above waterline
remedies.notes: Failed or missing anti-siphon valve allows water to back-flood when engine is stopped.

remedies.action: Check exhaust seacock is closed when engine not in use
remedies.notes: On some installations the exhaust outlet can allow back-flooding in a seaway.

entries.symptom: Intermittent loss of raw water flow — temperature spikes then recovers

remedies.action: Inspect raw water strainer — may be partially blocked and clearing itself with flow
remedies.notes: Strainer builds up debris that intermittently restricts and clears. Clean at every engine check.

remedies.action: Inspect raw water pump impeller — vanes may be cracking and intermittently blocking pump
remedies.notes: Degrading impeller vanes can collapse and partially block the pump then release, causing intermittent symptoms.

remedies.action: Check seacock for partial closure or debris on the inlet screen
remedies.notes: Ball valves can be inadvertently partially closed or have debris lodged against the inlet.

entries.symptom: Fuel consumption significantly higher than normal — no power change

remedies.action: Check for external fuel leak — inspect all connections, lift pump, injector lines
remedies.notes: A slow fuel leak can waste significant quantities without obvious symptoms. Smell for diesel and check bilge.

remedies.action: Check bottom for fouling — heavy growth significantly increases fuel consumption
remedies.notes: A fouled hull can increase fuel consumption by 20-40%. Often overlooked.

remedies.action: Have injectors tested — worn injectors that dribble waste fuel
remedies.notes: Dripping injectors increase consumption and cause black smoke. Easy to diagnose with flow test.

entries.symptom: Engine misfiring under load — rough running, occasional bang or stumble at speed
entries.frequency_signature: irregular combustion events

remedies.action: Replace spark plugs and check HT leads on petrol engine
remedies.notes: Fouled or worn spark plugs cause misfiring under load. Replace as a set.

remedies.action: Test injectors on diesel — have spray pattern and opening pressure checked
remedies.notes: A misfiring cylinder on diesel is almost always an injector issue. Compression test to rule out mechanical.

remedies.action: Check for water contamination in fuel
remedies.notes: Water in fuel causes misfiring and stumbling, especially at higher loads.

entries.symptom: Banging or clanking from under the boat when running — goes away at low speed
entries.frequency_signature: irregular banging at speed

remedies.action: Check for fouled propeller or object wrapped around shaft — stop engine immediately
remedies.notes: Rope, net, or debris around the shaft is the most common cause of sudden banging from aft.

remedies.action: Inspect propeller blades for strike damage causing imbalance and cyclical impact
remedies.notes: A badly bent blade causes severe vibration and banging at speed. Boat should not be run until prop is checked.

remedies.action: Check engine mounts — collapsed mount allows engine to move and knock against bearers
remedies.notes: Completely failed mount lets the engine shift under load, causing banging from engine bay rather than aft.

entries.symptom: Helm feels heavy or steering is stiff — no hydraulic leak visible

remedies.action: Check and top up hydraulic steering fluid — air in system causes stiffness
remedies.notes: Low fluid from small leak or air ingress causes heavy steering. Bleed system after topping up.

remedies.action: Inspect rudder bearings and pintles for wear or seizure
remedies.notes: Worn or corroded rudder fittings cause increasingly heavy helm, especially noticeable at slow speed.

remedies.action: Check tiller arm and steering cable connections for binding or corrosion
remedies.notes: Cable steering systems corrode internally. Full-lock-to-lock travel should be smooth and even.

entries.symptom: Acrid burning smell from engine bay — no smoke visible

remedies.action: Check drive belts for slipping or glazing — slipping belt smells strongly of burning rubber
remedies.notes: Slipping belt is the most common cause of burning smell with no visible smoke.

remedies.action: Inspect wiring looms for chafing or overheating cables
remedies.notes: Burning plastic smell. Check areas where cables may chafe against engine. High priority — fire risk.

remedies.action: Check if anything (rags, bilge foam, debris) has fallen onto exhaust manifold
remedies.notes: Objects on hot exhaust are a common cause of burning smell with no obvious electrical fault.

entries.symptom: Diesel engine will not start — cranks normally but refuses to fire

remedies.action: Bleed air from the fuel system — crack injector unions while cranking to purge air
remedies.notes: Air in fuel is the most common cause of no-start on diesel. Even a small air lock prevents starting.

remedies.action: Check fuel level and make sure correct fuel valve is open
remedies.notes: Simple checks first — empty tank or closed valve will prevent starting.

remedies.action: Test glow plugs — a diesel won't start when cold with all plugs failed
remedies.notes: Failed glow plugs prevent cold starting. Test individually and replace any that don't heat up.

entries.symptom: Excessive condensation or water ingress into engine compartment — rust forming on components

remedies.action: Improve engine bay ventilation — check vents are clear and add ventilation if inadequate
remedies.notes: Poor ventilation causes condensation that accelerates corrosion. Engine bays need both intake and exhaust ventilation.

remedies.action: Check for deck leaks above engine bay — seal hatches, inspection ports, and deck fittings
remedies.notes: Water ingress from above is often mistaken for condensation. Check after rain.

remedies.action: Run engine regularly to warm the bay and dry moisture — corrosion worsens on boats left unused
remedies.notes: Regular use is the best corrosion prevention. Cold unused engine bays collect moisture.

entries.symptom: Boat vibrates at a specific RPM band — fine above and below, rough through a narrow range
entries.frequency_signature: resonant vibration at specific RPM, typically 1200-1800 RPM

remedies.action: Identify and pass through the resonant RPM range — all hulls have them, not necessarily a fault
remedies.notes: Every boat has natural hull frequencies that amplify vibration at certain RPMs. If it has always done this, it may be normal.

remedies.action: Replace engine mounts — worn mounts allow resonant frequencies to transmit to hull more easily
remedies.notes: Stiffened or deteriorated mounts no longer isolate engine vibration and make resonant bands worse.

remedies.action: Check propeller pitch and have it adjusted — incorrectly pitched prop can excite hull resonance
remedies.notes: Changing propeller pitch shifts the resonant RPM band to a different speed — sometimes all that is needed.

entries.symptom: Coolant level dropping but no visible external leak — no overheating yet

remedies.action: Check for head gasket failure — look for mayo emulsion under oil cap and exhaust smoke
remedies.notes: Coolant disappearing internally into combustion is early head gasket failure. Catch it before overheating destroys the head.

remedies.action: Pressure test cooling system — check for small external leaks at hoses, clips, and heat exchanger
remedies.notes: Small external leaks may only drip when the system is hot and under pressure, making them hard to spot visually.

remedies.action: Inspect mixing elbow and wet exhaust hose for internal seepage
remedies.notes: A weeping mixing elbow can lose coolant internally into the exhaust system.

entries.symptom: Vibration felt through hull when in neutral at dock — not present when in gear
entries.frequency_signature: vibration at idle RPM, disappears under load

remedies.action: Check idle speed setting — too low an idle causes vibration on some engines
remedies.notes: Raising idle speed slightly often reduces or eliminates dock vibration.

remedies.action: Check engine mounts — a mount may be collapsing on one side only
remedies.notes: One failed mount causes the engine to sit at an angle, transmitting vibration unevenly.

remedies.action: Check for air in fuel or partially blocked injector on one cylinder — causes rough idle
remedies.notes: Rough idle from uneven cylinder contribution causes hull vibration in neutral.

entries.symptom: Loud slapping noise from engine — sounds like something loose inside
entries.frequency_signature: irregular slapping, not strictly RPM-proportional

remedies.action: Stop engine immediately and check oil level — do not restart without investigation
remedies.notes: A sudden slapping noise that is not RPM-proportional may indicate a loose component inside the engine. Running risks catastrophic damage.

remedies.action: Check for a broken or loose heat shield or engine cover that is vibrating against engine
remedies.notes: Loose external metalwork can produce convincingly internal-sounding noise. Check before assuming the worst.

remedies.action: Have engine inspected by a marine engineer — borescope inspection of cylinders recommended
remedies.notes: If external causes are ruled out, internal inspection is required before the engine is run again.

entries.symptom: Wet exhaust dripping water from hose joints or mixing elbow

remedies.action: Replace wet exhaust hose and check all jubilee clip positions — hose deteriorates from inside
remedies.notes: Marine wet exhaust hose has a short service life. Internal carbon buildup and swelling causes leaks at joints.

remedies.action: Replace mixing elbow — cast elbows corrode through from inside over several years
remedies.notes: Mixing elbows are consumable items. Most manufacturers recommend replacement every 5 years on salt water use.

remedies.action: Check exhaust throughhull and silencer connections for cracking or joint failure
remedies.notes: Fibreglass silencers crack, especially around pipe entry points. Inspect in good lighting.

entries.symptom: Engine runs hot only in shallow water or near seabed — fine in deep water

remedies.action: Check raw water inlet — in shallow water, sediment, silt, and weed can block the seacock inlet screen
remedies.notes: Very common in tidal harbours and anchorages. Raw water strainer should always be checked after shallow water operation.

remedies.action: Clean seacock inlet strainer and increase monitoring frequency in shallow or silty areas
remedies.notes: Operational practice — slow down in very shallow water if overheating tendency is known.

entries.symptom: Strong diesel smell in the cabin — not from exhaust

remedies.action: Inspect all fuel lines for weeping joints, cracked hose, or loose clips — check when engine is warm
remedies.notes: Diesel fuel smell in cabin is a safety concern. Find and fix the source before using the boat.

remedies.action: Check fuel tank breather — a blocked breather causes fuel to be pushed through tank seams
remedies.notes: Blocked tank breather creates vacuum/pressure that can push fuel through small leaks.

remedies.action: Inspect injector leak-off pipes and unions for seepage
remedies.notes: High-pressure injector pipes and return pipes can seep at unions, causing smell without obvious dripping.

entries.symptom: Gearbox will not engage — lever moves but no drive in forward or reverse

remedies.action: Check cable/linkage connection at gearbox — may have come off or snapped
remedies.notes: Gear cable failure is the most common cause of sudden loss of gear selection. Check before opening gearbox.

remedies.action: Check gearbox oil level — some boxes will not engage if oil is very low
remedies.notes: Hydraulic gearboxes rely on oil pressure for clutch engagement. Low oil = no drive.

remedies.action: Check gearbox hydraulic pressure — may need a marine engineer with a pressure gauge
remedies.notes: Internal clutch or hydraulic pump failure inside gearbox — specialist diagnosis required.

entries.symptom: High frequency whining or buzzing from engine bay — distinct from belt noise, constant pitch
entries.frequency_signature: high frequency constant pitch hum or buzz

remedies.action: Inspect raw water pump impeller and housing — worn housing causes cavitation noise
remedies.notes: A worn pump housing allows the impeller to slip, causing a constant high-frequency whine.

remedies.action: Check for air ingestion in raw water system — air causes cavitation hum
remedies.notes: Air leak at seacock or strainer connection causes pump to cavitate and produce buzzing sound.

remedies.action: Inspect fuel lift pump — diaphragm failure causes buzzing as pump struggles to deliver
remedies.notes: Failing lift pump produces a buzzing or chattering sound at idle.

entries.symptom: Engine temperature runs cooler than normal — thermostat area cold

remedies.action: Replace thermostat — a stuck-open thermostat prevents the engine reaching operating temperature
remedies.notes: Thermostat stuck open is almost always the cause of running too cool. Cheap and quick to fix.

remedies.action: Check temperature gauge sender for accuracy — sender failure can give false low readings
remedies.notes: Check gauge with a different sender before doing any other work.

entries.symptom: Unusual vibration only at full throttle — fine at part throttle
entries.frequency_signature: vibration at high RPM only

remedies.action: Inspect propeller for minor blade damage or fouling that only loads at high RPM
remedies.notes: Small prop imbalance or damage is only felt under high load. Inspect after every season.

remedies.action: Check shaft coupling bolts for looseness — high RPM can reveal coupling play
remedies.notes: Coupling bolts work loose over time and cause vibration that is most noticeable at high speed.

remedies.action: Check engine mounts are all equally loaded — one loose mount allows movement at high RPM
remedies.notes: All four mounts should be equally tensioned. A loose mount lets the engine rock at full power.

entries.symptom: Oil consumption noticeably increased — topping up every few hours

remedies.action: Check for external oil leaks — inspect rocker cover gasket, sump gasket, and all oil seals
remedies.notes: External leaks are common and obvious on close inspection. Look for oil staining on engine and below.

remedies.action: Check for oil burning — blue smoke from exhaust, especially on start-up or deceleration
remedies.notes: Oil burning means worn rings, valve seals, or turbo seals. Run a compression test to assess severity.

remedies.action: Check PCV/crankcase breather system — blocked breather pressurises crankcase and forces oil past seals
remedies.notes: Blocked crankcase breather is a common, cheap cause of oil consumption and external leaks.

entries.symptom: Engine starts and runs for a few seconds then cuts out — repeating

remedies.action: Check fuel supply — fuel may be running out, valve may be closed, or tank pickup may be blocked
remedies.notes: Engine runs on fuel in the lines then stops when supply fails. Basic checks first.

remedies.action: Bleed air from diesel fuel system thoroughly
remedies.notes: Air locks allow engine to start and run until the air pocket reaches injectors, then it dies.

remedies.action: Check for overheating — engine cuts out on thermal protection if coolant or oil temperature is too high
remedies.notes: Check temperature gauges and alarm history. Some engines have automatic cut-out protection.

entries.symptom: Severe shaking of the whole boat at anchor in neutral — engine running normally
entries.frequency_signature: whole-hull resonance at idle

remedies.action: Check all engine mount condition — one or more may have completely failed
remedies.notes: A fully collapsed mount allows direct transmission of engine vibration to hull, causing whole-boat shaking.

remedies.action: Check shaft coupling alignment — recent shaft work can leave alignment incorrect, exciting hull resonance
remedies.notes: Misaligned coupling causes vibration at idle that transmits through shaft into hull.

remedies.action: Have engine idle speed checked and adjusted
remedies.notes: Idle speed at an unfortunate RPM can excite hull natural frequency. Moving idle speed slightly often helps.

entries.symptom: Exhaust emits sparks or glowing particles at night

remedies.action: Check for dry exhaust — loss of water injection into exhaust allows soot to ignite
remedies.notes: Sparking exhaust usually means raw water is not entering the exhaust — check impeller and raw water flow immediately.

remedies.action: Have injectors tested — overfuelling from worn injectors produces unburned carbon that ignites in exhaust
remedies.notes: Heavy carbon deposits in a dry/hot exhaust can ignite. Sparking exhaust is a fire risk.

entries.symptom: Battery draining overnight with engine off — nothing obvious left on

remedies.action: Perform a current draw test — measure DC draw with everything supposedly off, isolate circuits one by one
remedies.notes: Use a multimeter in series on the negative terminal. Any draw over 50mA with everything off indicates a fault.

remedies.action: Check bilge pump for continuous running or stuck float switch
remedies.notes: A stuck float switch keeps bilge pump running continuously, draining battery overnight.

remedies.action: Check battery condition — old batteries lose capacity rapidly and appear to drain when they are simply worn out
remedies.notes: A battery over 4 years old should be load-tested. Most marine battery failures look like unexplained discharge.

entries.symptom: Injector clatter — sharp metallic ticking from engine, more prominent when cold
entries.frequency_signature: sharp tick, RPM-proportional, from injector area

remedies.action: Have injectors tested for opening pressure — injectors that open at wrong pressure cause diesel knock
remedies.notes: Injector clattering is diesel knock from incorrect injection timing or pressure. Common on older engines.

remedies.action: Check injection pump timing — retarded timing causes hard knock especially when cold
remedies.notes: Timing can drift on older mechanical injection pumps. Cold knock that reduces when warm often points to timing.

remedies.action: Use correct specification diesel fuel — biodiesel blends and poor-quality fuel cause increased knock
remedies.notes: Low cetane diesel or heavily blended biodiesel causes diesel knock. Premium marine diesel reduces this.

entries.symptom: Fuel in the bilge — strong smell and sheen on bilge water

remedies.action: Do not run the engine or any electrical equipment — evacuate if petrol. Ventilate thoroughly.
remedies.notes: SAFETY CRITICAL. Petrol vapour in bilge is an explosion risk. Diesel is less volatile but still a fire risk. Find and fix the leak before resuming engine use.

remedies.action: Trace the fuel source — check all fuel lines, tank connections, and fuel filter housings
remedies.notes: Common failure points: fuel hose at tank pickup, filter bowl O-ring, lift pump diaphragm.

entries.symptom: Speedometer reading erratically or not at all — GPS speed is correct

remedies.action: Remove and clean paddle wheel transducer — often fouled with growth or debris
remedies.notes: Paddle wheel fouling is the most common cause of erratic or zero speed reading.

remedies.action: Check transducer connections and cable for corrosion or damage
remedies.notes: Corroded connections at the back of the instrument or at the transducer cause intermittent readings.

remedies.action: Replace paddle wheel or transducer unit if above don't resolve
remedies.notes: Transducer bodies crack and paddle wheels break. Replacement is inexpensive and easy to self-fit.

entries.symptom: Turbocharger making a high-pitched whine — louder on acceleration
entries.frequency_signature: high-pitched whine increasing on throttle application

remedies.action: Check turbo oil feed and return pipes for blockage — turbo must have clean oil supply
remedies.notes: Insufficient lubrication causes turbo bearing noise. Always let a turbocharged engine idle for 2 minutes before shutdown.

remedies.action: Inspect turbo intake for collapsed hose or blockage causing noise from restricted airflow
remedies.notes: Partially collapsed intake hose causes whistle or whine that increases with demand.

remedies.action: Have turbo inspected for shaft play or bearing wear by a specialist
remedies.notes: Turbo bearing failure produces increasing whine then eventual failure. Shaft should have no radial play.

entries.symptom: Boat will not plane — engine reaches WOT RPM but boat stays in displacement mode

remedies.action: Check propeller pitch — pitch too high prevents engine reaching WOT and planing
remedies.notes: Engine should reach manufacturer's WOT RPM range. Too much pitch prevents this and stops the boat planing.

remedies.action: Check boat bottom for fouling — heavy growth prevents planing even with adequate power
remedies.notes: A badly fouled hull can add enormous drag. Clean bottom is essential for planing performance.

remedies.action: Adjust trim — incorrect trim angle can prevent planing despite adequate power
remedies.notes: Too much bow-up trim prevents the boat getting over the hump. Adjust tabs or drive trim.

remedies.action: Check weight distribution and total load — overloaded boat may not plane in certain conditions
remedies.notes: Heavy load, especially aft, prevents planing. Redistribute weight forward and reduce load.
